name: kaiju-db

metadata.name: kaiju-db
classifiers: License :: OSI Approved :: Apache Software License

documentation:
```{image} https://badge.fury.io/py/kaiju-db.svg
:alt: Latest package version
:target: https://pypi.org/project/kaiju-db
```

```{image} https://img.shields.io/badge/code%20style-black-000000.svg
:alt: Code style - Black
:target: https://github.com/psf/black
```

[Python](https://www.python.org) >=3.11 [Postgres](https://www.postgresql.org) >=14

Database connector services, database initialization, fixtures initialization, migrations.
Interfaces for remote interaction with tables via RPC.
See [documentation](https://kaiju-db.readthedocs.io/) for more info.

# Installation

```console
pip install kaiju-db
```

You need a [kaiju application](https://gitlab.com/kaiju-python/kaiju-base-app) to run these services.

class_variables.name: kaiju_db.types.MigrationState.commands
documentation: : a list of sql commands performed inside a transaction block

Migration service migration.

This data format is used by migration services to perform migrations. See an example below.

```python
{
    "id": 0,
    "comments": "dev comments",
    "commands": [
        "ALTER TABLE my_table ADD COLUMN new_col DEFAULT NULL;",
        "ALTER TABLE my_table DROP COLUMN old_col;",
    ]
}
```

modules.name: kaiju_db.functions

functions.name: kaiju_db.functions.DDL
documentation:
Specifies literal SQL DDL to be executed by the database. DDL objects
function as DDL event listeners, and can be subscribed to those events
listed in {class}`.DDLEvents`, using either {class}`.Table` or
{class}`.MetaData` objects as targets. Basic templating support allows
a single DDL instance to handle repetitive tasks for multiple tables.

example create function before create table task:

```python
DDL(
    task,
    "before_create",
    """
        CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION TaskSortingIncrement()
        RETURNS trigger AS $BODY$BEGIN
           NEW.sort:= (SELECT COALESCE(max(sort), 0) FROM task WHERE status_id = NEW.status_id) + 1;
           RETURN NEW;
        END
        $BODY$
        LANGUAGE plpgsql VOLATILE
        COST 100;
    """
)
```

example create trigger after create table task:

```python
DDL(
    task,
    "after_create",
    """
        DROP TRIGGER IF EXISTS TaskTriggerBeforeInsert
        ON task;
        CREATE TRIGGER TaskTriggerBeforeInsert
        BEFORE INSERT
        ON task
        FOR EACH ROW
        EXECUTE PROCEDURE TaskSortingIncrement();
    """
)
```

```
param target

table

param identifier

see doc https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/13/core/event.html

param body

sql
```

variables.name: kaiju_db.functions.SQL_FUNCTIONS
documentation: : sql functions registry

classes.name: kaiju_db.functions.UserFunction

class_variables.name: kaiju_db.functions.UserFunction.body
documentation: : executable sql string

Function interface for SQL functions.

```python
class MyFunction(UserFunction):
    package = 'utils'
    name = 'my_func'
    type = sa.types.Integer
    body = "my_func(v integer) RETURNS integer AS $$ BEGIN RETURN v; END; $$ LANGUAGE PLPGSQL;"

    def __init__(self, v: int):
        return super().__init__(v)
```

Now you can use this function from python. See `https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/13/core/functions.html`
for detail about generic functions methods.

```python
await self.app.db.fetchval(MyFunction(42).select())
```

To make your function actually available in your app you need to tell
the database service about it, i.e. register it in the function registry

```
from kaiju_db.services import functions_registry

functions_registry.register_class(UserFunction)
```

or (if you have many)

```
from kaiju_db.services import functions_registry

import my_functions

functions_registry.register_from_module(my_functions)
```

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.DatabaseService.engine
documentation:
Get sqlalchemy async engine.

See `sqlalchemy.AsyncEngine <https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/20/orm/extensions/            asyncio.html#sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio.AsyncEngine>`_

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.DatabaseService.begin
documentation:
Start an sqlalchemy transaction with auto-commit.

See [sqlalchemy.AsyncEngine.begin](https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/20/orm/extensions/asyncio.html#sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio.AsyncEngine.begin)

```python
async with db.begin() as conn:
    ...  # transaction block
```

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.DatabaseService.connect
documentation:
Start an sqlalchemy transaction with no auto-commit.

See [sqlalchemy.AsyncEngine.connect](https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/20/orm/extensions/asyncio.html#sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio.AsyncEngine.connect)

```python
async with db.connect() as conn:
    ...  # transaction block
    await conn.commit()
```

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.DatabaseService.execute
documentation:
Execute a single SQL command.

A wrapper around `sqlalchemy.AsyncConnection.execute <https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/20/orm/extensions/            asyncio.html#sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio.AsyncConnection.execute>`_

This method will return a `sqlalchemy.Result object <https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/20/core/            connections.html#sqlalchemy.engine.Result>`_ so you can use `result.all()` or `result.first()` etc.
    to retrieve results (these methods are synchronous) depending on what you need. These methods return
    *namedtuple rows*. To convert a namedtuple to a old-style dictionary use `row._asdict()` method.

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.DatabaseService.fetchrow
documentation:
Execute an SQL command and fetch the first row.

A wrapper around `sqlalchemy.AsyncConnection.executoe <https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/20/orm/extensions/            asyncio.html#sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio.AsyncConnection.execute>`_

Returns the first row as dict or None.

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.DatabaseService.fetch
documentation:
Execute an SQL command and fetch all the results.

A wrapper around `sqlalchemy.AsyncConnection.executoe <https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/20/orm/extensions/            asyncio.html#sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio.AsyncConnection.execute>`_

Returns a list of rows as dicts.

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.DatabaseService.fetchval
documentation:
Execute an SQL command and fetch the first column of the first row.

A wrapper around `sqlalchemy.AsyncConnection.execute <https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/20/orm/extensions/            asyncio.html#sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio.AsyncConnection.execute>`_

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.DatabaseService.add_table
documentation:
Register a table in the database service metadata.

This method must be called before async init if you want to automatically create this table at start time.
SQL services do this automatically for their tables.

Postgresql database transport service.

Initializes a connection pool and can provide connections and basic execution commands. It also can perform initial
database, functions and tables initialization.

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.SQLService.get_condition_hook
documentation:
Set up specific get conditions.

You can use this hook to impose permission check based on current user session, add some conditions
to filter out data for public (rpc) output etc.

Example:

```python
def get_condition_hook(self, sql):
    sql = sql.where(self.table.c.enabled.is_(True))
    return sql
```

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.SQLService.insert_condition_hook
documentation:
Set up specific insert conditions.

Similar to {py:meth}`~kaiju_db.services.SQLService.get_condition_hook`.

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.SQLService.update_condition_hook

Set up specific update conditions.

Similar to {py:meth}`~kaiju_db.services.SQLService.get_condition_hook`.

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.SQLService.delete_condition_hook
documentation:
Set up specific delete conditions.

Similar to {py:meth}`~kaiju_db.services.SQLService.get_condition_hook`.

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.SQLService.exists
documentation:
Return True if object exists. False otherwise.

```
param id

object id
```

If you have composite primary keys you should pass them as tuples:

```python
await service.exists((1, 'abc'), ...)
```

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.SQLService.m_exists
documentation:
Return a set of existing IDs for a list of IDs.

```
param id

list of object ids

returns

a set of existing ids
```

:::{note}
Be aware that there is database limit on max number of ids per query (65535 in postgres).
:::

If you have composite primary keys you should pass them as tuples:

```python
await service.m_exists([(1, 'abc'), ...])
```

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.SQLService._sql_get_column
documentation: Return an SQLAlchemy column by its name.

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.SQLService._sql_get_columns
documentation:
Construct a list of columns from their names.

```python
'*'             # all columns
"..."           # single column
["...", "..."]  # multiple columns
```

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.SQLService.get
documentation:
Return information about an object.

```
param id

object id

param columns

list of columns to return,  for no return,  for all

raises NotFound

```

If you have composite primary keys you should pass them as tuples:

```python
await service.get((1, 'abc'), ...)
```

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.SQLService.m_get
documentation:
Return multiple objects.

```
param id

object id

param conditions

filtering conditions.

param columns

list of columns to return,  for no return,  for all

returns

a list of objects found
```

:::{note}
Be aware that there is a database limit on max number of ids per query (65535 in postgres).
:::

Objects that don't exist will be skipped.
Returns all data at once without pagination. Use `SQLService.list` if
you want pagination or sorting.

If you have composite primary keys you should pass them as tuples

```python
await service.m_get([(1, 'abc'), ...], ...)
```

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.SQLService.delete
documentation:
Remove a single object from a table.

```
param id

object id

param columns

list of columns to return,  for no return,  for all

returns

a deleted object columns if requested

raises NotFound

if object doesn't exist or already was deleted
```

If you have composite primary keys you should pass them as tuples:

```python
await service.delete((1, 'abc'), ...)
```

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.SQLService.m_delete
documentation:
Remove multiple objects from a table. Non-existing objects will be skipped.

```
param id

object ids

param conditions

filtering conditions.

param columns

list of columns to return,  for no return,  for all

returns

a list of deleted objects if requested
```

If you have composite primary keys you should pass them as tuples:

```python
await service.m_delete([(1, 'abc'), ...])
```

You can also use conditions without primary keys if you provide an empty key list:

```python
await service.m_delete([], conditions={'enabled': False})
```

Prepare data before insert.

```
raw data
```

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.SQLService.create
documentation:
Create a single object.

```
param data

objects data

param columns

columns to return,  for no return,  for all

param on_conflict

on conflict clause if required ('do_nothing', 'do_update')

list of on conflict constraints

param on_conflict_values

an object with on conflict values, used only by  clause

param _connection

optional connection object (when using inside a transactional block)

returns

inserted object if requested
```

Customize {py:meth}`~kaiju_db.services.SQLService.prepare_insert_data` to normalize and prepare data
before insert if you want more control over inserted data.

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.SQLService.m_create
documentation:
Create multiple objects.

```
param data

list of objects data

param columns

columns to return,  for no return,  for all

param on_conflict

on conflict clause if required ('do_nothing', 'do_update')

param on_conflict_keys

list of on conflict constraints

param on_conflict_values

an object with on conflict values, used only by  clause

param _connection

optional connection object (when using inside a transactional block)

returns

inserted objects if requested
```

Customize {py:meth}`~kaiju_db.services.SQLService.prepare_insert_data` to normalize and prepare data
before insert if you want more control over inserted data.

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.SQLService.prepare_update_data
documentation:
Prepare data before update.

```
param data

raw data
```

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.SQLService.update
documentation:
Update a single object. Raises error if object doesn't exist.

```
param id

object id

param data

data to update

param columns

columns to return,  for no return,  for all

param _connection

optional connection object (when using inside a transactional block)

returns

inserted object if requested

raises NotFound

```

Customize {py:meth}`~kaiju_db.services.SQLService.prepare_update_data`
to normalize and prepare data before update if you want more control over updated data.

Use tuples for composite keys:

```python
await service.update((1, 'abc'), ...)
```

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.SQLService.m_update
documentation:
Update multiple objects with the same data. Non-existing objects will be skipped.

```
param id

list of ids

param data

update data

param columns

columns to return,  for no return,  for all

param conditions

update conditions

param _connection

optional connection object (when using inside a transactional block)

returns

inserted objects if requested
```

Customize {py:meth}`~kaiju_db.services.SQLService.prepare_update_data`
to normalize and prepare data before update if you want more control over updated data.

Use tuples for composite keys:

```python
await service.m_update([(1, 'abc'), ...], ...)
```

You can also use conditions without primary keys if you provide an empty key list:

```python
await service.m_update([], data={'value': 42}, conditions={'enabled': True})
```

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.SQLService.after_update_hook
documentation:
Execute after update service-specific logic.

The method is called after each update or m_update query with the rows which has been updated. It is executed
only if rows have been returned by the update method. The rows would contain only the columns specified
by `columns` arg of the query.

One can use this method to implement cache or event/notification system for SQL services.

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.SQLService.after_create_hook
documentation:
Execute after create service-specific logic.

The method is called after each create or m_create query with the rows which has been updated. It is executed
only if rows have been returned by the create method. The rows would contain only the columns specified
by `columns` arg of the query.

One can use this method to implement cache or event/notification system for SQL services.

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.SQLService._create_conditions
documentation:
Add SQL conditions to sql query.

Allows some simple queries to be constructed.

```python
[ ... ]  # OR
{ ... }  # AND

{ "...": "..." }            # ==
{ "...": ["...", "..."] }   # IN
```

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.SQLService._create_sort
documentation:
Add SQL ordering command to a query.

```python
"..."            # sort ASC by a key
{"desc": "..."}  # sort DESC by a key
[ ..., ... ]     # sort by multiple keys in order
```

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.SQLService._create_pagination
documentation:
Add pagination directives to an SQL query.

Limit < 1 transforms to 1 and offset < 0 transforms to 0 by default.

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.SQLService._create_count_query
documentation: Use COUNT(id) for slow precise count or TABLESAMPLE for estimate.

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.SQLService._get_page_count
documentation: Return current page number and number of pages.

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.SQLService.list
documentation:
List rows with pagination and conditions.

```
param conditions

optional query conditions

param sort

optional row ordering

param offset

optional row offset

param limit

optional row limit

param count

calculate page count

param columns

columns to return,  for nothing (count only),  for all columns

param _connection

optional connection object (when using inside a transactional block)

returns

This method may return different data depending on the provided params
```

Condition example:

```python
service.list(
    conditions={
        'tag': ['tag_1', 'tag_2', 'tag_3'],  # IN condition
        'active': True,                      # EQ condition
        'value': {'gt': 41, 'le': 42'},      # num conditions,
        'text': {'like': 'sht'},             # text field "likeness"
    }
)
```

Available numeric conditions: gt, lt, ge, le, eq
Available other conditions: like

Sort example:

```python
service.list(
    sort=['tag', {'desc': 'timestamp'}]     # order matters
)
```

Available sorting conditions: desc, asc (default)

You can use this method for counting without returning any results.
Just set the limit to zero. Optionally, you can also set the counting
precision.

```
service.list(
    conditions={ ... },
    precision=50
    limit=0
)
```

Contrary, if you don't need counting, you can disable it. No count / page
data will be available then.

```
service.list(
    conditions={ ... },
    count=False
)
```

Precision uses a number of table samples to estimate the count. If
the precision is set to 0 or None, then the exact count will be performed.

:::{attention}
Precision is not working at the moment (don't know why).
:::

If count argument is False, then count, page and pages result values
will be None.

If columns is None, then data will be None and on_page will be zero.

```python
{
    count: Optional[int]          #: total rows matching the query, None if count hasn't been requested
    offset: int                   #: row offset for this selection
    page: Optional[int]           #: current page number, None if count hasn't been requested
    pages: Optional[int]          #: total pages, None if count hasn't been requested
    on_page: int                  #: number of rows on this page
    data: Optional[List[dict]]    #: returned rows, None if limit was set to 0
}
```

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.SQLService.iter
documentation:
Iterate over listed data.

```
param conditions

optional query conditions

param sort

optional row ordering

param offset

optional row offset

param limit

optional row limit

param columns

columns to return,  for all columns

returns

This method may return different data depending on the provided params
```

Same as {py:meth}`~kaiju_db.services.SQLService.list` except it provides an async generator over all selected
data. At each iteration a batch with max size of `limit` is returned. You can use this method to
asynchronously iterate over selected table data in chunks.

```python
async for batch in sql_service.iter(conditions={'enabled': True}, sort=['created'], limit=100):
    for row in batch:
        ... # batches of max 100 rows will be returned
# the generator will exit after when last batch of data has been returned
```

class_variables.name: kaiju_db.services.SQLService.DEFAULT_ROW_LIMIT
documentation: : defaults row limit on list / iter queries

class_variables.name: kaiju_db.services.SQLService.MAX_ROW_LIMIT
documentation: : max page size in list / iter queries

class_variables.name: kaiju_db.services.SQLService.select_columns
documentation: : you can specify a whitelist of output columns here

class_variables.name: kaiju_db.services.SQLService.update_columns
documentation: : you may specify columns for update here

documentation: : here should be your table

class_variables.name: kaiju_db.services.SQLService.virtual_columns
documentation: virtual SQL columns (i.e. functions) names and definitions

inner_classes.name: kaiju_db.services.SQLService.ErrorCode
documentation: These error codes should be commonly used for SQL related errors.

Base SQL service interface with common commands and errors.

Optimized for a single primary key only with a name "id"

Example of use:

You need to set your own table and, if needed, define column whitelists
for different operations.

```python
class MyService(SQLService):
    table = my_table
    select_columns = {'id', 'data', 'flag'}
    insert_columns = {'data', 'flag'}
    update_columns = {'flag'}
```

Here you can access some basic methods: "exists", "get", "create", "update",
"delete" and their bulk versions (for multiple objects at once).

You can change any of SQL base for these commands by redefining query
constructors.

```python
class MyService(SQLService):

    ...

    def _create_get_query(id, columns, table=None):
        ... # custom query

    def _create_m_get_query(self, id: list, columns, table=None):
        ... # custom query
```

```
param app

web app instance

param database_service

database service instance or instance name

param logger

optional logger instance
```

classes.name: kaiju_db.services.FixtureService

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.FixtureService.__init__
documentation:
Initialize.

```
param app

web app

param root_dir

fixtures base dir

param fixtures

list of fixtures (service names) to load, None - load all

param empty_tables_only

load only when the service table is empty

param load_on_init

load all fixtures on service init (when starting the app)

param logger

optional logger instance
```

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.FixtureService.load_all
documentation: Load all fixtures from the root dir.

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.FixtureService.load_fixture
documentation:
Load a particular fixture.

```
param path

fixture path, must contain a json array of objects
```

Fixture service.

It can load data from json files using SQLService interface.

How to set up automatic fixtures:

1. Add FixtureService to your app configuration.
2. Create your sql service.
3. Create a .json file inside `FixtureService.root_dir` location with the name matching your sql service
    public name, i.e. `your_service.service_name`.
4. Write an array of json objects into the file with object attributes same as your service expects
    in `m_create` method.

Upon the app start the fixture service will automatically create data from fixture files *if there is no data
currently in respective tables*.

classes.name: kaiju_db.services.DatabaseMigrationService

Initialize.

```
param app

default migrations file

param migrate_on_start

perform an automatic migration from the current state at async init

param logger

```

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.DatabaseMigrationService.migrate
documentation:
Migrate DB from one state to another.

```
param from

state to migrate from (by default the current state is used)

param to

state to migrate to (by default the last state is used)

param migrations_file

optional migrations file path

return

current state id
```

methods.name: kaiju_db.services.DatabaseMigrationService.get_state
documentation: Get current (supposed) database state.

Simple migration tool.

This service does not resolve migration issues leaving them for the developer. You should create an appropriate
SQL queries yourself before migration. The only thing what this service does is maintains the database state
and performs listed migration instructions from a pre-configured list of migrations.
The service uses `db_info` table to store its current state.

How to set up migrations:

1. Add `DatabaseMigrationService` to your configuration file.
2. Create `DatabaseMigrationService.migrations_file` which must be a json array of migration state objects.
3. When a migration is required you should write a new migration state into a json array,
   see {py:class}`~kaiju_db.types.MigrationState` for example. Note that `id` must be an incremental integer
   starting from 0.

Once the app has started, the migration service will automatically perform all the migrations.

inner_classes.name: kaiju_db.services.PermHook.Permission
documentation:
User permissions.

These keys will be joined with <service_name> to create unique permission keys for each
service.

Sql service with a pre-configured user permissions hook.

it uses 'user_id' column by default to check if objects can be edited or viewed by users.
You should modify `_set_user_condition()` if you intend to use different columns.
